narrative_blocks.header: Died at thirty-one; widow renamed herself Whitton
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Hooker died March 25, 1866 in Indianapolis at thirty-one — leaving Clementine Russell Hooker with four children under eight, the youngest, Henry Samuel, barely a year old — and within a few years she had remarried, becoming Clementine Russell Hooker Whitton, a name that preserved and then buried her first husband's in the same breath. She outlived him by eleven years, dying in 1877; their son James Harvey survived to 1931, their daughter Margaret to 1952, meaning Henry Hooker's children outlasted him by decades in the same Indianapolis streets where he had died young.

narrative_blocks.header: He died in the army — details unknown
narrative_blocks.body: George Hooker was born Christmas Eve 1838 in Buffalo, New York, and EH records his death with the terse notation 'd. in the army' — meaning he died during Civil War service, though whether by battle, disease, accident, or wounds is not specified. He was one of at least two soldiers in this immediate family; his uncle Gilbert Hooker had served in the Quartermaster's Department in the Revolutionary War, and George died young in the army of the republic that war had made.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

burial.plot_notes: Crown Hill Cemetery Indianapolis per FA

military_service.unit: Company H, 12th Regiment, Indiana Volunteer Infantry
military_service.notes: Sgt. Major, Company H, 12th Indiana Infantry. Same company as PVT John Brooks (I01044), whose widow Louisa Vanzant Jack later married. Post-war: Stockbridge Marble Co., Massachusetts per FA.

narrative_blocks.header: He married the widow of his company's Private
narrative_blocks.body: Sergeant Major John 'Jack' Hooker served in Company H of the 12th Indiana Volunteer Infantry during the Civil War; Private John Brooks served in the same company and died April 22, 1864 at Scottsboro, Alabama, buried at Chattanooga National Cemetery. After the war ended, Jack Hooker married Brooks's widow, Louisa Vanzant — a connection that collapsed the distance between survival and loss into a single family, the sergeant major and the dead private joined permanently through the woman who had belonged to both.

narrative_blocks.body: Marquis R. Hooker died March 20, 1934 in Indianapolis at fifty-three, killed by a skull fracture suffered in a fall from scaffolding to a cement floor — possibly at Tomlinson Hall, Indianapolis's famous civic and market venue, though the specific work site is unconfirmed. His death by industrial accident at the height of the Depression ended a life that had begun in the city his grandfather Azel Hooker and great-grandfather Gilbert Hooker had never seen, but which had become home to three generations of the family over sixty years of Indiana settlement.
